metadata.title: Build a Candidate DAG and Audit It with Claude

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: I want to go from a vague mental model to a defensible DAG and then immediately stress its weak points. Given a problem I describe, build the candidate graph and audit it. Specifically: (1) draft the DAG with every node and edge justified in one line, (2) run an audit that checks for cycles, unjustified edges, and omitted common causes, and (3) mark the two edges whose removal or reversal would most change my identification strategy so I know what to defend first.

props.command: I want to go from a vague mental model to a defensible DAG and then immediately stress its weak points. Given a problem I describe, build the candidate graph and audit it. Specifically: (1) draft the DAG with every node and edge justified in one line, (2) run an audit that checks for cycles, unjustified edges, and omitted common causes, and (3) mark the two edges whose removal or reversal would most change my identification strategy so I know what to defend first.
props.segment: Build a Candidate DAG and Audit

beats.narration: You describe four variables in plain English. Claude draws the DAG — and you immediately find the edge it invented.
A DAG is a falsifiable hypothesis about causal structure, not a fact. The auditing step — finding the invented edge — is the whole point of having Claude propose it.
We describe a five-variable marketing funnel: ad spend, website traffic, product quality, customer satisfaction, and repeat purchase. Claude proposes the DAG.

props.command:
claude "For this system:
  ad_spend, traffic, quality,
  satisfaction, repeat_purchase
propose a DAG as directed edges.
For each edge: one-sentence justification.
Flag plausible omitted confounders."

beats.narration: Claude proposes eight edges. One is suspicious: quality directly causes traffic. That is plausible for SEO in the long run, but not in the short run — it is likely an invented shortcut confusing reputation effects with direct traffic causation.

props.code:
# dag_proposal.py — Claude output
edges = [
    ('ad_spend',    'traffic'),       # paid traffic
    ('ad_spend',    'satisfaction'),  # <- suspicious
    ('quality',     'traffic'),       # <- invented?
    ('quality',     'satisfaction'),  # direct mechanism
    ('traffic',     'satisfaction'),  # engagement
    ('satisfaction','repeat_purchase'),# loyalty
    ('quality',     'repeat_purchase'),# direct
    ('ad_spend',    'repeat_purchase'),# retargeting
]
# Flagged confounders: brand_strength
# (affects both ad_spend and quality)

beats.narration: The DAG has eight proposed edges. The student critique log names one invented edge — quality causes traffic — one missing edge: brand strength affects both quality investment and ad spend. One plausible confounder: brand strength, flagged by Claude itself.
We add brand strength as an explicit confounder to the variable list and ask Claude to revise the DAG. Are its edges structural or just correlational?

props.command:
claude "Add variable: brand_strength.
Revise DAG. For each new edge involving
brand_strength: is it a structural
mechanism or just correlation?"

beats.narration: Claude adds brand-strength-to-quality and brand-strength-to-ad-spend with structural justifications — brand equity enables higher quality investment, and high-brand firms spend more on ads. The student verifies both are mechanistic, not merely correlational.
A Claude-proposed DAG is a starting point for critique, not a final answer. The value is in what you disagree with — every rejected edge is a testable claim about what does not cause what.
Next: apply the three-failure taxonomy to two LLM-generated DAGs for the same marketing problem.
Like and subscribe for more causal reasoning with Claude.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Build a Candidate DAG and Audit It with Claude] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
